evidence.title: Management of Low Back Pain: Guidelines From the VA/DoD | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Low back pain is one of the most frequently experienced medical conditions, affecting 84% of adults in the United States at some point. Nearly 40% of adults will have experienced this type of pain within a three-month period. Low back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and U.S. Department of Defense (VA/DoD) have published guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of low back pain.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old adult presenting with low back pain that started about one week ago. The pain is described as sharp, located along the spine, and radiates slightly to the right buttock. The patient reports no numbness, weakness, or tingling in the legs or feet, no bladder or bowel dysfunction, and no systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. The pain worsens with prolonged sitting. The onset may be related to helping move heavy objects about a week ago. The patient has not tried any medications but has applied ice to the area.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute mechanical low back pain likely related to musculoskeletal strain.
Differential:
1. Lumbar strain or sprain
2. Radiculopathy without neurological deficit
3. Early degenerative disc disease
4. Sacroiliac joint dysfunction

P: - First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen 500 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: No known allergies, no renal or liver disease, not pregnant; monitor for gastrointestinal side effects with NSAIDs
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if new onset of numbness, weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or worsening pain occurs; seek emergency care if severe neurological symptoms develop
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags and neurological deficits
